summary: Gruber reviews the iPhone 5 as the nicest object he owns, emphasizing its unprecedented build quality, lighter aluminum unibody design, and micron-level precision manufacturing. He explores the tradeoffs of the taller 4-inch display, acknowledging it improves video and reading but compromises one-handed reachability. He contrasts the enthusiastic consumer response with the tech press's collective yawn, arguing the gap exists because 'niceness' defies benchmarks and specs. The review covers LTE performance (nearly indistinguishable from Wi-Fi), camera improvements especially in low light, and computing power that exceeds any PowerPC laptop Apple ever made. Gruber concludes the iPhone 5 represents Apple's fractal refinement of the same core idea, improved year after year in infinitely finer detail.
key_points: The iPhone 5's aluminum unibody is lighter, more durable, and more premium-feeling than the glass-backed 4/4S, making it the nicest object Gruber owns — stated without exaggeration
The taller 4-inch display is a reasonable compromise but hurts one-handed use: back buttons and status bar taps require re-gripping, like windshield wipers that can't reach the far corner
A growing chasm exists between excited consumers and a yawning tech press because niceness has no benchmark or spec — but you can feel it
LTE on Verizon is nearly indistinguishable from Wi-Fi, prompting Gruber to switch carriers after years on AT&T
The iPhone 5's Geekbench score exceeds every PowerPC laptop Apple ever built — pocket computing now surpasses 2003 professional desktop workstations
money_quotes: This iPhone 5 review unit is the single nicest object in my possession. I own things that cost and remain worth more. But I own nothing this nice.
There is no benchmark, no tech spec, to measure nice. But you can feel it.
The bored-by-the-iPhone tech press/industry experts surely value niceness, but they do not hold it in the same top-tier regard that Apple does.
Using the iPhone 5 on LTE is nearly indistinguishable from using it on Wi-Fi.
Consider the windshield wipers on a car, and how, because they swing in a radial arc, they can't reach the passenger-side top corner. Using the iPhone 5 is like that.
Without selling a single dedicated 'camera' since the groundbreaking but discontinued-in-1997 QuickTake, Apple has become one of the leading camera companies in the world.
Apple is simply improving upon that idea year after year in infinitely finer detail, like a fractal. It's nice.
I believe many people would choose poorly. Bigger looks better.
key_insight: The iPhone 5's defining quality is a 'niceness' of build and materials that no spec sheet can capture but every hand can feel, explaining why consumers line up while the tech press yawns.
